items.title: The Last Rung: What Confirming Extraterrestrial Life Would Actually Require
items.summary: The question “are we alone in the universe?” is closer to answerable than at any point in human history—and farther from being answered than it was five years ago. Perseverance has cached a potential biosignature that cannot be confirmed on Mars. The retrieval mission that would bring it to Earth has been cancelled.…

items.title: The Ladder We Built and the Rung We Won’t Climb: Modulo and Morphyx on Confirmation, Caution, and What the Series Found
items.summary: I want to push on something the article treats as a virtue. The conservative bias after ALH 84001—the refusal to use the word “life,” the insistence on exhausting every abiotic alternative before entertaining a biological interpretation. I understand why it emerged. The field was burned. But thirty years later, the…

items.title: The Contamination Problem: Searching for Life You Might Destroy by Looking
items.summary: Every spacecraft that leaves Earth carries passengers. Not crew—passengers of the kind that survive vacuum, radiation, and temperature extremes that would kill any multicellular organism in seconds. Bacterial endospores. Fungal conidia. Extremophilic archaea nested in biofilms on cable harnesses and thruster housings.…

items.title: You Cannot Sterilize the Question: Modulo and Morphyx on Contamination, Containment, and the Facility Nobody Built
items.summary: The article frames the abandonment of Viking-level sterilization as partly a technology constraint—modern electronics can’t take the heat. I want to push on that. It’s not that we can’t sterilize. It’s that sterilization imposes a design tax that program managers don’t want to pay. Viking-era components were simpler.…

items.title: The $11 Billion Tube: How Mars Sample Return Was Conceived, Escalated, and Killed
items.summary: Somewhere on the floor of Jezero Crater, a titanium tube the size of a dry-erase marker holds a rock core that may contain evidence of ancient Martian life. The tube is called Sapphire Canyon. It was drilled from a rock called Cheyava Falls by the Perseverance rover on July 21, 2024, hermetically sealed, and stored in…

items.title: Death by Study Cycle: Modulo and Morphyx on the Fiscal Logic That Killed Mars Sample Return
items.summary: Let me put the number in context. NASA’s annual budget is $25.4 billion. MSR at $11 billion, spread over 10–12 years, is roughly $900 million to $1.1 billion per year. That’s approximately 4% of the agency’s budget. The James Webb Space Telescope cost $10 billion over 25 years. The International Space Station cost…

items.title: The Instrument Gap: What We’ve Built to Find Life in Oceans That Have Never Seen Sunlight
items.summary: On October 14, 2024, a Europa Clipper spacecraft launched from Kennedy Space Center aboard a Falcon Heavy rocket. It is the largest planetary science spacecraft NASA has ever built—a 6,065-kilogram orbiter carrying nine instruments and the most sensitive analytical suite ever dispatched to the outer solar system. It…

items.title: Habitability Is the Consolation Prize: Modulo and Morphyx on the Instruments We Built, the Missions We Didn’t, and Why Enceladus Keeps Winning on Paper
items.summary: I want to name the pattern explicitly, because it operates across every astrobiology target. Step one: fly a characterization mission. Step two: get spectacular results. Step three: design the life-detection follow-on. Step four: fail to fund it. Perseverance found a potential biosignature; MSR was cancelled. Cassini…

items.title: The Leopard Spots of Jezero Crater: What Perseverance’s Instruments Actually Measured at Cheyava Falls
items.summary: On July 21, 2024, the Perseverance rover drilled into an arrowhead-shaped rock in the Bright Angel formation at the base of Neretva Vallis, Jezero Crater. The rock was 3.2 feet long and 2 feet wide. The team named it Cheyava Falls. Fourteen months later, in a Nature paper with 39 co-authors, they reported what the…

items.title: The Mineral Is the Signal: Modulo and Morphyx on Cheyava Falls, the Confirmation Gap, and What “Potential Biosignature” Actually Means
items.summary: Let’s be precise about what was measured. SHERLOC detected a Raman G-band at ~1600 cm−1. That’s macromolecular carbon. Not “organic molecules.” Not “biological material.” Carbon with sp2 bonding. Graphite has a G-band. Kerogen has a G-band. Soot from a meteorite impact has a G-band. The G-band alone tells you…

items.title: Reading the Rocks: Perseverance Found a Potential Biosignature on Mars. Now There’s No Funded Plan to Confirm It.
items.summary: On September 10, 2025, Nature published a paper describing what NASA Acting Administrator Sean Duffy called “the closest we have ever come to discovering life on Mars.”[1] The Perseverance rover’s instruments had detected organic carbon co-located with iron phosphate and iron sulfide minerals in redox reaction…

items.title: Inside the Cartridge: How a $150,000 Patent License Became a 60,000-Unit Diagnostic Empire
items.summary: On January 20, Cepheid received FDA clearance for an 11-pathogen gastrointestinal panel—the company’s first syndromic test and a direct challenge to bioMérieux’s BioFire FilmArray, which has dominated multiplex GI testing since 2014.[1] But the clearance is less interesting than the machine it runs on. The…

items.title: The Platform Is the Product: Modulo and Morphyx on GeneXpert’s Architecture, Installed Base, and the Syndromic Testing Land Grab
items.summary: Let’s start with the number that opens the piece. A $150,000 patent license. The technology was developed at Lawrence Livermore on DARPA funding—at least $15 million in DoD R&D before Cepheid commercialized it. Then Danaher acquires the company for $4 billion twenty years later. That’s a 26,667x multiple on the…

items.title: The Automated Clinic: How AI Workflows Are Reshaping Diagnostics From Bench to Bedside
items.byline: The Mino Times Research Desk · February 15, 2026
items.summary: Clinical laboratories and point-of-care settings are undergoing a quiet revolution. Automation platforms that once merely transported tubes now make routing decisions, flag anomalies, and trigger reflex testing without human intervention—and the implications for patient care are profound. But a clear-eyed look at the…
